Patch Set 1: Code-Review+1

Patch Set 1: Code-Review-1

-1 until this is clarified.

Patch Set 1:

> So why would you consider this kernel comment is wrong?

There's nothing in this comment stating that SELinux setsecurity depends on i_mutex. If you have an idea how things can go wrong, please share it. It's not the only place where Lustre drops i_mutex prior to entering sections usually called with i_mutex held, e.g. mm generic write routines (where i_mutex is replaced with range locks).

Patch Set 1:

Sorry, but this patch deliberately does the opposite from a comment stating that selinux_inode_notifysecctx() should be called with inode->i_mutex locked.

I am not saying this is wrong, but this patch has to justify why inode->i_mutex is not in fact necessary. For instance by explaining how Lustre makes use of LDLM to prevent any issue. Or by creating a new test in the test suite, that exercises this part of the code.

Comments should also be added in the code where calls to inode_{lock,unlock} are removed. Otherwise, later on when looking at the code, we will be wondering why we are not taking inode->i_mutex despite the comments for selinux_inode_notifysecctx() in the Linux kernel. We should not have to git-blame to find out.

Patch Set 1:

> Sorry, but this patch deliberately does the opposite from a comment
 > stating that selinux_inode_notifysecctx() should be called with
 > inode->i_mutex locked.
 
The quoted comment does not state that it SHOULD be called with i_mutex held. It just states that it IS called with i_mutex held. It can well be a reminder not to attempt to lock i_mutex recursively. 

I agree that it makes sense to add more comments to the patch though.

Patch Set 1:

Alex, could you also add the "Fixes" tag with the commit id which implemented this deadlock?

Patch Set 1:

> > Sorry, but this patch deliberately does the opposite from a
 > comment
 > > stating that selinux_inode_notifysecctx() should be called with
 > > inode->i_mutex locked.
 > 
 > The quoted comment does not state that it SHOULD be called with
 > i_mutex held. It just states that it IS called with i_mutex held.
 > It can well be a reminder not to attempt to lock i_mutex
 > recursively.
 > 
 > I agree that it makes sense to add more comments to the patch
 > though.

That is a possible interpretation indeed. I wish this comment was less ambiguous :(

Could you please try to get confirmation of this by writing to the SELinux mailing list?

parents.subject: LU-6142 utils: Fix style issues for lfs.c

commit.subject: LU-13617 llite: don't hold inode_lock for security notify
commit.message:
LU-13617 llite: don't hold inode_lock for security notify

With selinux enabled client has a dead lock which leads to
client eviction from MDS.
1 thread                    2 thread
do file open                do stat
inode_lock(parend dir)
                            got LDLM_PR(parent dir)
enqueue LDLM_CW(parent dir) waits on inode_lock to notify security
waits
timeout on enqueue
and client eviction because client didn't cancel a LDLM_PR lock

security_inode_notifysecctx()->selinux_inode_notifysecctx()->
selinux_inode_setsecurity()
The call of selinux_inode_setsecurity doesn't need to hold
inode_lock.

Fixes: 1d44980bcb ("LU-8956 llite: set sec ctx on client's inode at create time")
Signed-off-by: Alexander Boyko <alexander.boyko@hpe.com>
Cray-bug-id: LUS-8924
Change-Id: I4727da45590734bde57bee9d378b61c30b5d515a
Reviewed-on: https://review.whamcloud.com/38792
Tested-by: jenkins <devops@whamcloud.com>
Reviewed-by: Sebastien Buisson <sbuisson@ddn.com>
Tested-by: Maloo <maloo@whamcloud.com>
Reviewed-by: Andrew Perepechko <andrew.perepechko@hpe.com>
Reviewed-by: Oleg Drokin <green@whamcloud.com>
